title: Large-$N$ scaling of Tan's contact for the harmonically trapped Tonks--Girardeau gas at finite temperature
abstract: We derive the canonical-ensemble scaling of Tan's contact for $N$ harmonically trapped Tonks--Girardeau bosons at finite temperature in the large-$N$ limit. The leading scaling coefficient reproduces the local-density-approximation result and is obtained from a contour-integral representation of the canonical partition function followed by a saddle-point reduction to a phase-space integral with a self-consistent scaled chemical potential. The subleading coefficient is the central new object of this work: it admits an explicit representation in terms of universal phase-space integrals of the Fermi factor, has closed-form Sommerfeld and virial limits, and is identified with the canonical-versus-grand-canonical ensemble difference at fixed mean particle number. In the high-temperature Boltzmann regime the ratio of subleading to leading coefficients collapses to a universal value, traceable to the Poissonian particle-number statistics of the dilute grand-canonical gas. We construct Padé approximants for both scaling functions that interpolate uniformly between the low-temperature Sommerfeld and high-temperature virial regimes; for the subleading coefficient we report a form that is uniformly accurate on our working range of temperatures and asymptotically correct beyond. The scaling law is verified against canonical contour-integration data across the full temperature range.
summary: This theoretical paper determines the large-$N$ scaling of Tan's contact for a harmonically trapped Tonks-Girardeau gas at finite temperature. It establishes a two-term scaling law, identifying the subleading correction as a quantifiable difference between canonical and grand-canonical ensemble calculations. The results are validated using advanced mathematical techniques like contour integration and Padé approximants across all temperature regimes.
main_problem: The paper derives the large-$N$ scaling law for Tan's contact, a measure of short-range correlations, for a harmonically trapped Tonks-Girardeau gas at finite temperature.
main_result: The contact exhibits a two-term large-$N$ expansion, $C_N(	au) = A(	au) N^{5/2} + B(	au) N^{3/2}$, where the subleading coefficient $B(	au)$ is identified as the ensemble difference between canonical and grand-canonical calculations.
method: The derivation employs contour integration techniques on the canonical partition function, followed by saddle-point approximations and the construction of Padé approximants to ensure uniform accuracy across different temperature regimes.
model_or_system: The system is $N$ identical bosons confined by a harmonic potential and interacting via a contact potential, analyzed in the Tonks-Girardeau limit, which maps the system to noninteracting spinless fermions.
key_observables: Tan's contact ($C$), the leading scaling coefficient $A(	au)$, and the novel subleading coefficient $B(	au)$.
important_parameters: The dimensionless temperature ratio $	au = T/T_F$, where $T_F$ is the Fermi temperature of the noninteracting gas.
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed in the large-$N$ limit, and the validity of the Padé approximants is assumed across the working temperature range.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the raw data for $C_N(	au)$ verifying the scaling law, and later show the constructed Padé approximants interpolating between the low-temperature Sommerfeld and high-temperature virial regimes.
paper_structure: The paper systematically derives the leading coefficient $A(	au)$ first, followed by the novel subleading coefficient $B(	au)$. It then compares canonical and grand-canonical ensemble formulations to identify $B(	au)$ as the ensemble correction, culminating in the construction and validation of Padé approximants for both scaling functions.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, quantitative understanding of finite-temperature corrections to universal correlation measures in strongly interacting quantum gases, bridging the gap between different thermodynamic ensembles.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Canonical-ensemble contact (5) as a function of the reduced temperature τ, computed from the contour-integral representation (9). Curves correspond to particle numbers N from 10 to 100 in steps of 10 (bottom to top). The data underlie the scaling-law verification of Eq. (12).

Figure 2: Low-τ verification of the canonical scaling law (12). The ratio RN defined in (68), with A(τ) and B(τ) obtained from the universal integral representations (32) and (67) using the self-consistent scaled chemical potential ξ(τ) from (26), is plotted on τ ∈[0.005, 0.1] for N = 10, . . . , 100. Convergence to unity (dashed line) confirms the leading and subleading scaling at low temperature, including the Sommerfeld coefficient a2 = 4 √

Figure 3: High-τ verification of the canonical scaling law: the same ratio RN as in Fig. 2, now plotted on τ ∈[5, 10]. The collapse onto unity (dashed line) confirms the asymptotic forms A(τ) ≃√τ/π3/2 from (51) and B(τ) ≃−√τ/π3/2 from (103) in the Boltzmann regime. Residual deviation from unity at the largest-N, highest-τ corner is dominated by the spatial- integral truncation at finite Mmax and shrinks systematically with increasing Mmax.

Figure 4: Full-τ scaling verification of Eq. (12) across τ ∈[0, 10] and N = 10, . . . , 100. (a) Ratio RN from (68), evaluated with the numerical scaling functions A(τ) and B(τ). (b) Ratio R(P) N from (185), evaluated with the Pad´e approximants AP(τ) and BP(τ) of (177) and (181). In both panels the curves collapse onto unity (dashed line), confirming the canonical scaling law from the degenerate (τ ≪1) to the Boltzmann (τ ≫1) regimes and the validity of the Pad´e approximants as closed-form substitutes for the universal integrals. Residual deviations at the smallest N reflect the genuine O � N1/2 next-to-subleading correction.

Figure 5: Universal scaling functions A(τ) (left panel) and B(τ) (right panel). Solid blue: numerical evaluation of the integral representations (32) and (67) using the self-consistent ξ(τ) from (26). Dashed black: Pad´e approximants (177) and (181). Dotted red: low-τ Sommerfeld expansions (39) and (91). Dotted green: high-τ virial expansions (51) and (103). The Pad´e approximants interpolate between the two asymptotic regimes over τ ∈[0, 10].
